items.title: Two correlated features collapse to one component
items.scenario: A dataset has height in cm and height in inches—perfectly collinear after centering. PCA on the 2×2 covariance matrix yields one component explaining ~100% variance (the shared height direction) and one near-zero component.
items.explanation: Redundant features share variance; PCA merges them into a single dominant direction, showing why PCA helps with multicollinearity.

items.title: Sensor panel with dozens of channels
items.scenario: A factory logs 50 vibration sensors with strong cross-correlation. An engineer centers the matrix, fits PCA with k=5, and retains ~92% total variance—cutting storage and speeding anomaly detection models.
items.explanation: Explained variance ratio justifies k; downstream models train on 5 components instead of 50 raw sensors.

items.title: Mixed-scale census features
items.scenario: Age (0–100) and annual income ($0–$500k) sit in the same table. PCA on raw covariance is dominated by income variance; after standardizing, correlation PCA balances both features in the first components.
items.explanation: Preprocessing choice changes principal directions—covariance vs correlation PCA is a modeling decision, not a cosmetic step.

items.title: 2D scatter after projecting Iris to PC1–PC2
items.scenario: The Iris dataset (4 features) is centered, PCA-fit, and plotted on the first two components. Setosa separates along PC2 while virginica and versicolor overlap somewhat—useful EDA even though PCA was not told the species labels.
items.explanation: Low-dimensional PCA plots reveal structure for exploration; they do not guarantee class separation because PCA optimizes variance, not labels.
